# UC-10.15.4 · Credential Stuffing Burst Detection vs Baseline (MLTK)

## Description

Credential stuffing attacks generate sudden spikes in failed authentication from distributed sources. By baselining normal failure rates per application endpoint and flagging statistical outliers, this detection catches automated credential replay before account lockouts cascade.

## Value

Credential stuffing attacks generate sudden spikes in failed authentication from distributed sources. By baselining normal failure rates per application endpoint and flagging statistical outliers, this detection catches automated credential replay before account lockouts cascade.

## Implementation

Aggregate authentication failures in 5-minute windows per application. Compare to 30-day rolling baselines built with MLTK DensityFunction. Credential stuffing signatures include: high failure count, many distinct source IPs, and rapid succession. Enrich with threat intelligence on source IPs via ES. Trigger adaptive response actions (IP block via firewall API, CAPTCHA enforcement) when z-score exceeds threshold. Correlate with successful logins from the same source pool to detect compromised accounts. Feed risk events to RBA on both source IPs and targeted accounts.

## Detailed Implementation

Prerequisites
• Install and configure the required add-on or app: Splunk Machine Learning Toolkit (MLTK), Splunk Enterprise Security (ES).
• Ensure the following data sources are available: `index=main sourcetype=WinEventLog:Security` (EventCode=4625), web application auth logs, `index=web sourcetype=access_combined`.
• For app installation, inputs.conf, and Splunk directory layout, see the Implementation guide: docs/implementation-guide.md

Step 1 — Configure data collection
Aggregate authentication failures in 5-minute windows per application. Compare to 30-day rolling baselines built with MLTK DensityFunction. Credential stuffing signatures include: high failure count, many distinct source IPs, and rapid succession. Enrich with threat intelligence on source IPs via ES. Trigger adaptive response actions (IP block via firewall API, CAPTCHA enforcement) when z-score exceeds threshold. Correlate with successful logins from the same source pool to detect compromised ac…

Step 2 — Create the search and alert
Run the following SPL in Search (then save as report or alert; adjust time range and threshold as needed):

```spl
| tstats `security_content_summariesonly` count FROM datamodel=Authentication
    WHERE Authentication.action="failure"
    BY _time span=5m, Authentication.app, Authentication.src
| `drop_dm_object_name(Authentication)`
| eventstats avg(count) as baseline_avg stdev(count) as baseline_std by app
| eval z_score=round((count - baseline_avg) / nullif(baseline_std, 0), 2)
| where z_score > 4
| stats sum(count) as total_failures dc(src) as unique_sources max(z_score) as peak_z by _time, app
| where unique_sources > 10 AND total_failures > 100
| fit DensityFunction total_failures unique_sources into cred_stuffing_model
| where isOutlier > 0
| table _time, app, total_failures, unique_sources, peak_z
| sort -total_failures
```

Understanding this SPL

**Credential Stuffing Burst Detection vs Baseline (MLTK)** — Credential stuffing attacks generate sudden spikes in failed authentication from distributed sources. By baselining normal failure rates per application endpoint and flagging statistical outliers, this detection catches automated credential replay before account lockouts cascade.

Documented **Data sources**: `index=main sourcetype=WinEventLog:Security` (EventCode=4625), web application auth logs, `index=web sourcetype=access_combined`. **App/TA** (typical add-on context): Splunk Machine Learning Toolkit (MLTK), Splunk Enterprise Security (ES). The SPL below should target the same indexes and sourcetypes you configured for that feed—rename `index=` / `sourcetype=` if your deployment differs.

**Detection type** for this use case: source, app — interpret thresholds and fields in that context.

**Pipeline walkthrough**

• Uses `tstats` against accelerated summaries for data model `Authentication` — enable acceleration for that model.
• Invokes macro `drop_dm_object_name(Authentication)` — in Search, use the UI or expand to inspect the underlying SPL.
• `eventstats` rolls up events into metrics; results are split **by app** so each row reflects one combination of those dimensions.
• `eval` defines or adjusts **z_score** — often to normalize units, derive a ratio, or prepare for thresholds.
• Filters the current rows with `where z_score > 4` — typically the threshold or rule expression for this monitoring goal.
• `stats` rolls up events into metrics; results are split **by _time, app** so each row reflects one combination of those dimensions.
• Filters the current rows with `where unique_sources > 10 AND total_failures > 100` — typically the threshold or rule expression for this monitoring goal.
• Pipeline stage (see **Credential Stuffing Burst Detection vs Baseline (MLTK)**): fit DensityFunction total_failures unique_sources into cred_stuffing_model
• Filters the current rows with `where isOutlier > 0` — typically the threshold or rule expression for this monitoring goal.
• Pipeline stage (see **Credential Stuffing Burst Detection vs Baseline (MLTK)**): table _time, app, total_failures, unique_sources, peak_z
• Orders rows with `sort` — combine with `head`/`tail` for top-N patterns.

Step 3 — Validate
Compare a handful of scored rows to **the MLTK experiment** that produced the model, or to a manual replay of the same SPL on a short window, and confirm `fit` / `apply` output matches. For side-by-side, compare notable or entity context to **the ES Incident Review** queue if you feed risk or tickets.



Step 4 — Operationalize
Add the search to a dashboard or set up alert actions (email, webhook, PagerDuty, etc.) as required. Document the use case in your runbook and assign an owner. Consider visualizations: Line chart (failure rate vs baseline), Scatter plot (failures × unique sources), Table (burst events with z-scores).


## SPL

```spl
| tstats `security_content_summariesonly` count FROM datamodel=Authentication
    WHERE Authentication.action="failure"
    BY _time span=5m, Authentication.app, Authentication.src
| `drop_dm_object_name(Authentication)`
| eventstats avg(count) as baseline_avg stdev(count) as baseline_std by app
| eval z_score=round((count - baseline_avg) / nullif(baseline_std, 0), 2)
| where z_score > 4
| stats sum(count) as total_failures dc(src) as unique_sources max(z_score) as peak_z by _time, app
| where unique_sources > 10 AND total_failures > 100
| fit DensityFunction total_failures unique_sources into cred_stuffing_model
| where isOutlier > 0
| table _time, app, total_failures, unique_sources, peak_z
| sort -total_failures
```

## Visualization

Line chart (failure rate vs baseline), Scatter plot (failures × unique sources), Table (burst events with z-scores).
